journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of Muscle Research and Cell Motility

title-group.article-title: High-intensity interval training alleviates COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction via the BRD4/PGC-1α axis through restoring mitochondrial function and oxidative fiber composition

contrib-group.aff: https://ror.org/053v2gh09grid.452708.c0000 0004 1803 0208Department of Rehabilitation, The Second Xiangya Hospital of Central South University, No.139 Renmin Middle Road, Changsha, 410011 Hunan P.R. China

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Skeletal muscle dysfunction (SMD) associated with Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), characterized by muscle atrophy and altered fiber type distribution. High-intensity interval training (HIIT) is increasingly used clinically to improve cardiopulmonary and exercise functions in COPD patients, yet robust scientific evidence and exploration of its molecular mechanisms remain limited. In this research, bioinformatics analysis of GSE datasets revealed the differentially expressed genes in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues with COPD preliminarily. COPD rat models were established using cigarette smoke exposure. Cigarette smoke extract (CSE)-induced L6 myoblasts were used as cell models. It was found that BRD4 and PGC-1α were both significantly downregulated in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues with COPD and show a certain correlation. In vitro, BRD4 overexpression could restore PGC-1α levels and attenuate mitochondrial damage of L6 myoblasts caused by CSE treatment. HIIT significantly improved pulmonary function, muscle strength, and oxidative fiber composition in COPD rats by restoring the BRD4/PGC-1α axis and mitochondrial function, but the BET bromodomain inhibitor JQ1 could partially reverse the positive effects of HIIT. These findings not only provides some scientific evidence for the beneficial effects of HIIT on gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction caused by COPD but also suggest that the BRD4/PGC-1α axis plays functional roles in mediating the beneficial effects of HIIT on COPD-induced muscle dysfunction, highlighting its potential as a therapeutic target.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s10974-026-09723-4.

kwd-group.title: Keywords
kwd-group.kwd: Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma co-activator 1 alpha (PGC-1α)

funding-source.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of Hunan

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), which is the third-leading cause of death globally, is characterized by airflow limitation and persistent respiratory symptoms resulting from progressively increased chronic inflammatory response in the airways and the lungs to noxious particles or gases (Chen et al. 2024). COPD is also associated with skeletal muscle dysfunction, which results in poor life quality and exercise tolerance of patients (Spruit et al. 2013). Skeletal muscle dysfunction (SMD) is the most severe extrapulmonary complication in COPD patients, with an incidence rate of 15%-55% (Sepulveda-Loyola et al. 2020). SMD in COPD patients is characterized by muscle atrophy, decreased muscle strength, and altered muscle fiber type distribution, with a shift from oxidative to glycolyticfibers (Jiang et al. 2023). Managing SMD is of great importance; no specific drugs or targeted or personalized treatment strategies are available to improve the problem. The mechanisms underlying SMD have become a major topic of interest in the COPD Research field (Han et al. 2023). In our study, it was found that high-intensity interval training (HIIT) would restore the BRD4/PGC-1α axis, thereby promoting mitochondrial biogenesis and favorable muscle fiber type transformation, ultimately leading to significant improvements in both pulmonary and gastrocnemius muscle function in COPD rats.

Exercise training has been thought to be a promising therapeutic strategy for improving SMD in COPD patients in recent years. Among various exercise modalities, high-intensity interval training (HIIT) attracts increasing attention due to its potential benefits in enhancing cardiopulmonary function and skeletal muscle performance (Tzanis et al. 2017). HIIT typically involves repeated segments of high-intensity exercise interlaced with periods of low-intensity exercise or rest, which has been shown to induce significant improvements in aerobic capacity, muscle strength, and endurance in not only healthy individuals but also patients with various chronic diseases (Weston et al. 2014). Growing evidence demonstrates that HIIT may offer additional benefits over Moderate-intensity continuous training (MICT), which is another commonly used exercise regimen involving continuous exercise at a moderate intensity for a prolonged duration in terms of greater enhancements in skeletal muscle function and mitochondrial adaptations (Wang et al. 2025).

Bromodomain-containing protein 4 (BRD4), the best-characterized member of the bromodomain and extra-terminal (BET) family, functions as an epigenetic reader that recognizes acetylated lysine residues to orchestrate gene transcription and the chromatin landscape. It typically serves as a scaffold for transcription factors and is enriched at gene promoters and enhancers (Li et al. 2025). In addition, BRD4 is indispensable for the assembly of super-enhancers (SEs)—clusters of enhancers that drive high-level transcription of key genes and are marked by prominent H3K27ac deposition (Hua et al. 2022). Owing to its pivotal role in the pathogenesis of diverse disorders, including cancer, inflammatory diseases, fibrosis, and vascular pathologies, small-molecule BRD4 inhibitors such as JQ1 have been intensively investigated for therapeutic intervention (Li et al. 2025). Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma co-activator 1 alpha (PGC-1α) is a transcriptional coactivator that governs mitochondrial biogenesis and is tightly coupled to oxidative phosphorylation (Gelato et al. 2018). Transcriptomic dissection of the signaling network governing myofiber specification has identified PGC-1α as the master regulator driving the formation of oxidative, fatigue-resistant fibers (Ying et al. 2016). Comparing muscle composition between healthy controls and COPD patients, Harry et al. reported a obvious down-regulation of PGC-1α in the skeletal muscle of the latter, suggesting that reduced PGC-1α expression constitutes a key determinant of muscle dysfunction in COPD (Gosker et al. 2019). Notably, melanoma cells overexpressing PGC-1α exhibited a pronounced drop in PGC-1α transcript levels with BRD4 inhibition, implicating BRD4 as a critical upstream regulator of PGC-1α transcription (Gelato et al. 2018).

Until now, the underlying mechanisms through which HIIT exerts its beneficial effects on skeletal muscle function in COPD remain to be fully elucidated. In our study, an integrated bioinformatics analysis was performed on the original data from the public datasets GSE197463 and GSE18033. It was found that the transcriptional activator BRD4 and PGC-1α were significantly downregulated in COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle tissues with a correlation coefficient of 0.7656. BRD4 plays vital roles in COPD (Guo et al. 2023). PGC-1α is a key transcriptional co-activator that regulates muscle fiber type specification (Ringholm et al. 2013; Matsukawa et al. 2017). However, whether the BRD4/PGC-1α axis is involved in the skeletal muscle dysfunction pathogenesis of COPD and the therapeutic effects of HIIT remain to be investigated.

The present study aimed to investigate the effects of HIIT on pulmonary function, with a particular emphasis on gastrocnemius muscle function, in a rat model of skeletal muscle dysfunction induced by COPD. Special attention was given to the role of the BRD4/PGC-1α axis in mediating the beneficial effects of HIIT. We hypothesized that HIIT would restore the BRD4/PGC-1α axis, thereby promoting mitochondrial biogenesis and favorable muscle fiber type transformation, ultimately leading to significant improvements in both pulmonary and gastrocnemius muscle function in COPD rats.

sec.title: Establishment of the skeletal muscle dysfunction COPD models
sec.p: In this study, 5-month-old male Sprague Dawley rats (SD rats, purchased from Hunan Xu Zhi yuan He Biotechnology Co., Ltd.) were used to establish chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) models of skeletal muscle dysfunction. All rats were kept in a specific pathogen-free facility where standard food and water are freely available. Before the experiment performance, the rats were maintained in a standard breeding environment (23 ± 2 ◦C, a 12:12 h light: dark cycle) for one week of acclimatization. All the research protocols of our study were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Second Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, based on NIH Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (National Institutes of Health Publications No. 80 − 23, revised 1978), (approval No. 2021226).

SD rats were randomly divided into a negative control group (NC, n = 8) and a COPD model group (COPD, n = 24). The NC rats were exposed to fresh air, while the COPD rats were subjected to cigarette smoking exposure in an 8050 inhalation exposure system (6–8% of smog concentration, Tianjin, China). The smoke generated by commercially filtered cigarettes (trade name: Baisha, containing 0.8 mg of nicotine, 11 mg of tar, and 11 mg of carbon monoxide per cigarette) was directed into the system to achieve the condition of passive exposure. The COPD rats were daily subjected to two 1-h periods of CSE, twice per day at an interval of 6–8 h, 6 days per week for 24 weeks (Qin et al. 2022).

sec.title: Lung function evaluation
sec.p: The small animal lung function testing system (PFT-M, Beijing Twintech Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd., China) was used to analyze the lung function of the rats in different groups. In short, after the last CSE treatment had finished for 24 h, rats were fasted for 12 h and then anesthetized with 10% chloral hydrate. When the righting reflex and pain stimulus response disappeared, the rat trachea was cut open and connected to PFT for lung function testing after preparation and fixation. Measure forced vital capacity (FVC), forced expiratory volume at 200ms (FEV200), and maximum mid-expiratory flow rate (MMEF), and use them to evaluate lung function in the studied rats (Zhang et al. 2019; Su et al. 2020).

sec.title: Implementation of HIIT and MICT training
sec.p: After 5 days of treadmill adaptation training, the maximum exercise capacity test was conducted: the rats were introduced into the treadmill (tilted at a 15 ° angle and running at a speed of 15 m/min) and accelerated at a rate of 2.0 m/min every 2 min until they could not stand on the shock net even after receiving a 2-second electric shock. The training duration and maximum running speed (Vmax) were recorded. Under the same conditions (15 ° angle and running at a speed of 15 m/min), and then maintained it until the rat cannot stand on the shock net even after receiving a 2-second electric shock. Recorded the training duration. e highly selective BRD4 inhibitor JQ1 in vivo experiments which was given intraperitoneally at 30 mg/kg twice daily for 14 consecutive days, concurrently with a two-week HIIT training.

Both HIIT and MICT training were conducted 8 times within 2 weeks (Motiani et al. 2017). The HIIT training: Rats underwent 8 training sessions within 2 weeks, each consisting of 4 segments of 4-minute high-intensity training and 5 segments of 3-minute moderate-intensity training. The high-intensity training ran at 90% of maximum speed, while the moderate intensity training ran at 60% of maximum speed. Before and after the training, there was a 10-minute low-intensity training session at 40% of maximum speed as a warm-up. The HIIT single exercise volume was approximately 4 × 4 min × 90% × VO2max + 5 × 3 min × 60% × VO2max = 23.4VO2max. The MICT training consisted of 30 min of high-intensity running at 80% maximum speed. Before and after the training, there was a 10-minute low-intensity training with running at 40% maximum speed as a warm-up or relaxation. The MICT exercise volume per session was approximately 30 min × 80% × VO.max = 24.0 VO.max.

To explore if BRD4 is one of the molecular targets of HIIT in alleviating COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction, the highly selective BRD4 inhibitor JQ1 was given to the COPD rats intraperitoneally at 30 mg/kg twice daily for 14 consecutive days, concurrently with or without a two-week HIIT training.

sec.title: Measurement of relative muscle weight and Hind limb muscle strength
sec.p: After anesthesia, the rats were fixed on an anatomical plate with their abdomen facing upwards and their hind legs naturally extended. The skin and subcutaneous tissue were separated to expose the gastrocnemius muscle, which was carefully separated along the muscle fibers direction using forceps. Cut off the separated gastrocnemius muscle from the attachment point and soak it in physiological saline to keep the muscle tissue moist. After absorbing the moisture on the surface of the gastrocnemius muscle, its weight was accurately measured and recorded in a table. The relative weight of the gastrocnemius muscle (gastrocnemius muscle weight/body weight x 100%) was calculated.

After anesthetizing the rats, we fixed them on the experimental table with their abdomen facing upwards and their hind legs naturally extended. Placed the grip strength meter horizontally, with its grid facing towards the hind limbs of the rat. Gently supported the hind limbs of the rat, allowing its hind paws to naturally grasp the mesh of the grip strength meter, and ensured that the rat’s hind paws firmly grasped the grip strength meter. Grasped the end of the rat’s tail and gently pulled it back horizontally, maintaining even and increasing tension until the rat’s hind paw releasesed the grip strength gauge. Recorded the maximum force value of the rat’s hind paw grip, i.e., peak force. Performed 5 measurements on each rat and took the average as the muscle strength value of the rat’s gastrocnemius muscle.

sec.title: Histological staining and immunohistochemistry
sec.p: To assess pathological analysis, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was performed in the gastrocnemius tissues. The experimental steps were mainly based on the description of the previous reference literature (Su et al. 2020). Their morphologies were observed and photographed under panoramic scanning system (Panoramic MIDI, Hungary).

To investigate the changes in myofiber phenotypes in gastrocnemius muscle tissues to some extent, the immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining experiment was carried out as previously described (Ai et al. 2020). The MyHCI antibody and MyHCIIb antibody were added dropwise, and at the end of the experiment, all slides were observed and photographed under panoramic scanning system (Panoramic MIDI, Hungary).

sec.title: Construction of cell models
sec.p: The L6 myoblasts (CL-0136, Procell, authenticated by STR profiling) were cultured in DMEM (High glucose, PM150210, Procell), supplemented with 10% Penicillin-Streptomycin solution (PB180120, Procell), and 0.25 µg/mL of amphotericin B (no. 15240, Gibco) and 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS; no. 16420; Procell) until the cells reached about 90% confluence at 37℃(Abdelmoez et al. 2020; Gao et al. 2025).

L6 myoblasts were inoculated into a 6-well plate (2 mL/well) with a density of 2.0 × 105 cells/mL and incubated overnight in a 5% CO2 cell incubator at 37 ℃. When the cells reached approximately 70–80% confluence, a gain-of-function cell model of BRD4 was achieved by transfecting 4 µg of a BRD4 overexpression synthetic plasmid (generalbiol, China) into the cells using 5 µL of Lipotectamine-3000 (L3000008, invitrogen). After 48 h, the successful overexpression of BRD4 in L6 cells was confirmed by QPCR and western blot.

To prepare CSE solution for this study, an unfiltered cigarette containing 0.8 mg of nicotine and 11 milligrams of tar was lit, and the smoke was continuously drawn into PBS solution (10 ml) using a negative pressure suction device. After purification through a 0.22 μm filter, the solution was characterized as a 100% CSE solution. In order to further construct the COPD cell models, the L6 cells (NC-Ov, BRD4-Ov) were treated with above CSE solution at a final concentration of 10% for a predetermined duration (Li et al. 2023; Liu et al. 2022b).

sec.title: Bioinformatics analysis
sec.p: For the sake of explore the differentially expressed genes in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) preliminarily, we initially resorted to public datasets and achieve our goals through performing an integrated bioinformatics analysis of the data from the datasets GSE197463 (RNA-Seq transcriptomic data of gastrocnemius muscle tissues from wild type and COPD C57BL/6 mice. Con: n = 5, COPD: n = 5) and GSE18033 (Expression profiling by array data of gastrocnemius muscle tissues from wild type and COPD Female C57BL/6 mice, Con: n = 6, COPD: n = 6), resulting in a combined sample size of Con: n = 11 and COPD: n = 11. The bioinformatics analysis was standardized with COPD vs. Con as the comparison. ComBat was employed to adjust for batch effects across different platforms, encompassing probe-level microarray data and FPKM-converted RNA-seq expression values. Genes that meet the criteria of a P-value < 0.05 and |LogFC| > 1.0 were considered to have significant differences between the groups.

sec.title: Real-time quantitative PCR
sec.p: Total RNA was extracted from rat gastrocnemius muscle tissues and L6 cells using the RNAsimple Total RNA Kit (Cat. no. PD419, Tiangen, China), and then reverse-transcribed using the RevertAid First Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit (Cat. no. K1622, ThermoFisher, USA). The QPCR was performed in the Roche Light Cycler 480 system using SYBR Green Supermix (Cat.no.FP205, Tiangen, China). 3 independent experiments were carried out, and the relative quantification comparative Ct method was used to quantify the relative mRNA levels. The primer sequences were listed in Table 1. The QPCR measurement data are presented as the means ± SD (n = 3).

sec.title: Western blot assay
sec.p: Gastrocnemius muscle tissues and cells in each group were lysed. BCA Protein Assay Kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA, Cat# 23227) was used to measure the protein concentrations. Equal amounts of protein were separated with SDS-PAGE and then blotted onto PVDF membranes (Millipore, MA, USA, Cat# ISEQ00010). Next, the PVDF membranes were blocked in TBS containing 5% bovine serum albumin (BSA, Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA, Cat# A6003) and 0.05% Tween 20 for 1.5 h, then incubated at 4℃ overnight with the relevant primary antibodies. The primary antibodies used were listed in Table 2. The amount of the protein of interest was normalized to the densitometric units of GAPDH. After washing, the PVDF blots were incubated with HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies for 1 h. Protein expression was detected with ECL (Millipore, WBULS0500) by digital imaging systems. Finally, ImageJ software was used to analyze the band density and the statistical methods employed Student’s t-test. The WB densitometry data of protein bands are shown in Fig S1.

sec.title: Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP)
sec.p: The change in mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm), which is an important parameter reflecting mitochondrial activity, can be detected by the lipophilic fluorescent dye JC-1. In this study, Changes in MMP were determined using a JC-1 Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Assay Kit (HY-K0601, MCE, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. For cells, after JC-1 staining, cells with decreased mitochondrial activity showed green fluorescence, while normal cells showed red fluorescence. Fluorescence and red fluorescence can be detected in the FITC and PE channels, respectively. All samples were quantified for fluorescence intensity using FACSCalibur (BD Biosciences). The loss of MMP was indicated by the ratio of aggregates (red fluorescence) / monomer (green fluorescence). While for tissue samples, mitochondria were isolated from tissues and diluted to 0.1 mL (total protein 20–30 µg). JC-1 staining working solution (400-fold dilution) was prepared, and 0.9 mL was added to the mitochondrial samples. After mixing, 100 µL was added to a 96-well plate. Fluorescence was measured at Ex = 490 nm (green) and Ex = 530 nm (red) using a microplate reader. Mitochondrial membrane potential changes were analyzed using the red/green fluorescence ratio.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All experimental results listed in this research are expressed as the mean ± SD. GraphPad Prism 9.0 software (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA, USA) was used to perform for the statistical analysis. A t-test was carried out for the comparison of two conditions. ANOVA with a Bonferroni post-test was used for multiple comparisons. Statistical significance was assigned to P values below 0.05.

sec.title: In vivo and in vitro experiment design
sec.p: This study consists of two parts: in vivo and in vitro experiments. Figure 1 (Fig. 1) summarizes the experimental design for both in vivo and in vitro research, showing the groupings and treatment protocols used to investigate the effects of different interventions on COPD-induced muscle dysfunction.

caption.p: Experimental design for in vivo and in vitro studies.(a) Schematic representation of the experimental groups and treatment protocols for in vivo studies. (b) Schematic representation of the experimental groups and treatment protocols for in vitro studies

sec.title: HIIT significantly improves pulmonary function and gastrocnemius muscle function in COPD rats
Lung tissues from COPD rat models were subjected to hematoxylin–eosin staining for histopathological validation
sec.p: (Fig. 2a). To evaluate the therapeutic effects of HIIT on pulmonary and gastrocnemius muscle function in COPD rats, a 2-week training program with 8 sessions of HIIT in COPD model rats was conducted, and moderate-intense continuous training (MICT) served as the positive control. Forced expiratory volume in 200 milliseconds (FEV200) and forced vital capacity (FVC) were measured to assess pulmonary function. Results showed that FEV200, FVC, and ventilation per second in COPD rats were significantly lower than those in healthy rats (COPD vs. NC). After HIIT and MICT interventions, these indicators in COPD rats increased significantly compared to the COPD group, with HIIT showing more remarkable improvements than MICT when compared to the COPD group (COPD + HIIT vs. COPD) (Fig. 2b). We also measured training duration, hindlimb muscle strength, and relative gastrocnemius weight in the model rats. COPD rats had significantly lower values in these metrics than the healthy control (NC) group. HIIT - or MICT - trained COPD rats showed significant improvements in these muscle - function - related indicators compared to untrained COPD rats. Although no significant differences were found between the HIIT and MICT groups (COPD + HIIT vs. COPD + MICT), HIIT-trained COPD rats exhibited more substantial improvements in muscle function decline caused by COPD than MICT-trained rats (vs. COPD) (Fig. 2c).

caption.p: Effects of High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT) and Moderate-Intensity Continuous Training (MICT) on Pulmonary and Gastrocnemius Muscle Function in COPD Rats. (a) Histological structure of lungs in the control group (NC) and in the COPD model group. Magnification: × 200. (b) Lung volumes measured by forced expiratory volume in 200 milliseconds (FEV200), forced vital capacity (FVC) (left) and pulmonary ventilation function (rigth). Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance is indicated by *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001 betweem indicated two groups. (c) Training duration(left), hindlimb muscle strength (middle) and relative weight of gastrocnemius muscle (right) in different groups. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance is indicated by *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 betweem indicated two groups. (d) HE staining of gastrocnemius muscle tissues in different groups. Scale bar = 100 μm (left). Immunohistochemical staining for MyHCI (middle) and MyHCIIb (right) in gastrocnemius tissues. Scale bar = 100 μm. (e) percentage of IHC-positive cross-sectional area relative to total tissue cross-sectional area, ####p < 0.0001 compared to NC, *P < 0.05,***P < 0.001,****p < 0.0001 compared to COPD group. (f) QPCR and western blot analysis in gastrocnemius tissues of different groups, QPCR data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance is indicated by ####p < 0.0001 compared to COPD, *P < 0.05,** P < 0.01,****p < 0.0001 compared to COPD group

sec.p: Subsequently, HE staining was performed on the gastrocnemius muscle tissues of the model rats. It was suggested that the muscle fiber arrangement in the gastrocnemius muscle was regular and dense, the nuclei in muscle cells were evenly distributed, and no significant inflammatory infiltration was observed in the NC group. However, in COPD rats without HIIT or MICT, the gastrocnemius muscle tissue exhibited markedly thickened textures, increased and widened muscle fiber gaps, concentrated nuclear distribution in muscle cells, and apparent inflammatory infiltration when compared to the NC group. It is worth noting that in COPD rats subjected to HIIT or MICT training, these structural characteristics were attenuated to a certain extent (vs. COPD). In addition, immunohistochemical staining was performed for the slow-oxidative myofiber myosin heavy chain subtype marker MyHCI and the fast-glycolytic myofiber myosin heavy chain subtype marker MyHCIIb (the abundance of mitochondria and oxidative enzymes are lowest in Ⅱb fibers, which are very different from human muscles) (Pan et al. 2016; Bloemberg and Quadrilatero 2012) in gastrocnemius tissues. The results demonstrated that the COPD rat gastrocnemius tissue had significantly reduced MyHCI-positive staining and somewhat increased MyHCIIb-positive staining compared to the NC group, implying lower fatigue resistance (COPD vs. NC). While in COPD rats that received HIIT or MICT, the gastrocnemius tissues showed a marked increase in MyHCI-positive staining and a corresponding decrease in MyHCIIb-positive staining (vs. COPD) (Fig. 2d). The quantified fibre type (%) was presented as the positive cross sectional area / total cross sectional area which was calulated using ImageJ (Fig. 2e).

QPCR and Western blot were used to detect the myofiber heavy chain subtype markers MyHCI, MyHCIIa, and MyHCIIb expression in gastrocnemius tissue samples. TOMM20, a mitochondrial quality marker, was also included, as fiber type shifts involve changes in tissue mitochondrial content. Compared to the NC group, the COPD group exhibited reduced MyHCI and MyHCIIa expression but enhanced MyHCIIb expression. In contrast, in COPD rats that underwent HIIT or MICT, MyHCI and MyHCIIa expression was restored, and MyHCIIb expression was once again blunted in gastrocnemius tissues (vs. COPD) (Fig. 2f and g). These results suggested that both HIIT and MICT alleviated COPD-induced functional damage to the gastrocnemius to some extent. As for TOMM20, we also observed a significant decrease in expression in the COPD group (vs. NC), while the degree of TOMM20 abatement was also significantly alleviated in the COPD group with HIIT or MICT (vs.COPD) (Western blot quantification results are presented in Supplementary Material).

sec.title: The BRD4 / PGC-1α axis could play key roles in the functional loss of the gastrocnemius muscle of COPD rats
sec.p: This study aimed to identify the molecular targets through which high-intensity interval training (HIIT) ameliorates COPD-induced functional loss in the gastrocnemius muscle. we initially resorted to public datasets. Frustratingly, we failed to obtain any human or rat datasets that match our research purposes. We had no choice but to achieve our goals through performing an integrated bioinformatics analysis of the data of C57BL/6 mice from the datasets GSE197463 (Con: n = 5, COPD: n = 5) and GSE18033 (Con: n = 6, COPD: n = 6). Among the genes with significant differences (DEGs) between groups (Fig. 3a), we focused on key signaling molecules involved in myofiber synthesis and transcriptional regulatory factors. Considering the species differences between rats and mice, rat-specific QPCR primers were designed for some DEGs to validate and explore them in the COPD rat model established by our own. Among these genes, the transcriptional activator BRD4 and peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma co-activator 1 alpha (PGC-1α) attracted our attention. After batch effect correction using ComBat, analysis of the expression data from the public datasets revealed that both BRD4 and PGC-1α were significantly downregulated in COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle tissue compared with controls (Fig. 3b). Pearson correlation analysis showed a relative strong correlation between their expression levels, with a correlation coefficient of 0.7656 (Fig. 3c). To validate it in our established rat gastrocnemius muscle model, qPCR and Western blot were employed to detect the relative mRNA levels and protein expression of BRD4 and PGC-1α, respectively. The results demonstrated that both BRD4 and PGC-1α were significantly downregulated in the gastrocnemius muscles of COPD rats compared with the NC group (Fig. 3d, e and Fig. S1). Subsequently, we used L6 cells to construct a cell model of COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction by treating the cells with cigarette smoke extract (CSE) at a final concentration of 10%. We then validated the mRNA levels and proteins in this cell model using quantitative PCR (qPCR) and Western blotting. Our results demonstrated that the relative mRNA levels of BRD4,PGC-1α, MYH2(encoding MyHC IIa), MYH7 (encoding MyHC I) were significantly decreased after CSE treatment for 6 h, and the downregulation was more pronounced at 24 and 48 h. In contrast, MYH4 mRNA (encoding MyHC IIb) displayed an opposite trend. (Fig. 3f). The Western blot results were consistent with the qPCR findings. Additionally, in the cell model, it was observed that downregulation of MyHCI and MyHCIIa proteins and upregulation of MyHCIIb protein following CSE treatment compared with the negative control (NC). Meanwhile, the downregulation of MyHCI and MyHCIIa proteins became more significant with prolonged CSE intervention (Fig. 3g and Fig. S1 -3g). These results suggested that the downregulation of the BRD4 / PGC-1α axis may contribute to the dysfunction of the gastrocnemius muscle of COPD rats.

caption.p: Analysis of BRD4 and PGC-1α expression in COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction. (a) The clustering heatmap showing the differentially expressed genes in gastrocnemius muscle tissues from COPD groups compared to controls using the data from GSE197463 and GSE18033 (*p < 0.05, ∣logFC∣≥1.0, Up Top20 and Down all). (b) Box plot showing the expression levels of BRD4 and PGC-1α in gastrocnemius muscle tissue from COPD rats compared to controls after ComBat correction using the data from GSE197463 and GSE18033. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined by unpaired t-test (COPD vs.Con, *p < 0.05). (c) Scatter plot illustrating the correlation between BRD4 and PGC-1α expression levels in gastrocnemius muscle tissue from COPD group compared to controls. Pearson correlation coefficient (r^2) is indicated. (d) The relative mRNA leves of BRD4 and PGC-1α in rat gastrocnemius muscles (NC = 4, COPD = 5), data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined by T-tests (****p < 0.0001 vs. NC). (e) Western blot analysis of protein expression in rat gastrocnemius muscles (NC = 4, COPD = 5). (f) The relative mRNA levels of key factors in L6 cells treated with 10% CSE at different time points (6 h, 24 h, 48 h). Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s post-hoc test (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001 vs. NC). (g) Western blot analysis of protein expression in L6 cells treated with 10% CSE at different time points (6 h, 24 h, 48 h). β-actin was used as a loading control

sec.title: BRD4 restored the expression of PGC-1α and regulated the myogenic subtype transformation of CSE-induced L6 cells by attenuating mitochondrial damage
sec.p: To explore the biological function of the transcriptional activator BRD4 in CSE-induced L6 cells, we constructed a gain-of-function model of BRD4 in L6 cells (OV-BRD4) and treated the cells with 10% CSE for 48 h. Subsequently, the relative mRNA levels and protein expression of BRD4, PGC-1α, MyHCI, MyHCIIa and MyHCIIb were detected by QPCR and Western blot, respectively. The results showed that BRD4 was successfully overexpressed, at both the mRNA and protein levels (OV-BRD4 + CSE vs. OV-BRD4). Concurrently, the expression level of PGC-1α was increased with the BRD4 overexpression, regardless of CSE intervention. Notably, with the enhancement of the BRD4/PGC-1α axis, the expression levels of the two oxidative myofiber heavy chain subtype markers, MyHCI (encoded by MYH2) and MyHCIIa (encoded by MYH7), which were significantly downregulated by CSE intervention, were both significantly restored. Additionally, the expression of the glycolytic myofiber heavy chain subtype marker MyHCIIb (encoded by MYH4), which was significantly upregulated with CSE intervention, was also markedly reduced again (OV-NC + CSE vs. OV-BRD4 + CSE). To investigate whether the phenotypic transformation of the CSE-induced cell model caused by BRD4 overexpression was associated with improved mitochondrial status, the TOMM20 was also examined. Interestingly, the overexpression of BRD4 indeed increased TOMM20, which had been attenuated by CSE intervention (OV-NC + CSE vs. OV-BRD4 + CSE) (Fig. 4a-c). To further confirm this result, a mitochondrial membrane potential assay was performed. It was demonstrated that BRD4 overexpression did not cause significant changes in mitochondrial membrane potential without CSE treatment, as evidenced by the lack of significant differences in the fluorescence values and relative fluorescence intensity of 103 cells between the OV-NC and OV-BRD4 groups. However, under CSE intervention, BRD4 overexpression significantly increased the red fluorescence values and relative fluorescence intensity while correspondingly decreasing the green fluorescence values and relative fluorescence intensity (OV-NC + CSE vs. OV-BRD4 + CSE). The results suggested that BRD4 overexpression could restore the expression of PGC-1α and alleviate the mitochondrial damage caused by CSE treatment in L6 cells to some extent (Fig. 4d and e).

caption.p: BRD4 restores the expression of PGC-1α and regulates myogenic subtype transformation in CSE-induced L6 cells by attenuating mitochondrial damage. a, b The relative mRNA levels of BRD4, PGC-1α, MYH2, MYH4, MYH7 and TOMM20 in L6 cells overexpressing BRD4 (OV-BRD4) treated with or without CSE (10%) for 48 h. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined by one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s post-hoc test (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001 vs. OV-NC; #p < 0.05, ##p < 0.01, ####p < 0.0001vs OV-NC + CSE). c Western blot analysis of protein expression in L6 cells overexpressing BRD4 (OV-BRD4) treated with or without CSE (10%) for 48 h. β-actin was used as a loading control. d Flow cytometry analysis of mitochondrial membrane potential in L6 cells overexpressing BRD4 (OV-BRD4) treated with or without CSE (10%) for 48 h. The scatter plots show the fluorescence intensity of green and red fluorescence. e Bar graphs showing the fluorescence values and relative fluorescence intensity of 10^3 cells in L6 cells overexpressing BRD4 (OV-BRD4) treated with or without CSE (10%) for 48 h. The red bar represents red fluorescence, and the green bar represents green fluorescence. Data are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined by unpaired t-test (red, **p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001 vs. OV-NC + CSE; green, ###p < 0.0001, ####p < 0.0001vs OV-NC + CSE)

sec.title: High-intensity interval training (HIIT) partially alleviates COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction via the BRD4
sec.p: To explore whether BRD4 is one of the molecular targets of HIIT in alleviating COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction, the highly selective BRD4 inhibitor JQ1 was used. The results showed that in COPD rats subjected to HIIT, training duration, hindlimb standardized muscle strength, and relative gastrocnemius muscle weight were significantly improved compared with those in the group without HIIT intervention (COPD + HIIT vs. COPD). However, in rats that underwent HIIT and were simultaneously injected with JQ1, these three parameters showed significant declines, implying a reduction in the efficacy of HIIT (Fig. 5a). The relative mRNA levels and protein expression of BRD4 and PGC-1α were also detected. The results showed that these genes, which had significantly increased after HIIT intervention in COPD rats (COPD + HIIT vs. COPD), experienced a marked decline in the gastrocnemius muscle tissue of rats that underwent HIIT with JQ1 injection (COPD + HIIT + JQ1 vs. COPD + HIIT). The expression of myofibril heavy chain subtype markers in the gastrocnemius muscle tissue also changed accordingly. The oxidative myofibril markers MyHCI (encoded by MYH7) and MyHCIIa (encoded by MYH2), which had significantly increased after HIIT intervention (COPD + HIIT vs. COPD), declined again in the gastrocnemius muscle tissue of rats that underwent HIIT and were simultaneously injected with JQ1 (COPD + HIIT + JQ1 vs. COPD + HIIT). In contrast, the glycolytic myofibril marker MyHCIIb (encoded by MYH4), which had been suppressed after HIIT intervention (COPD + HIIT vs. COPD), increased again in the gastrocnemius muscle tissue of rats that underwent HIIT and were simultaneously injected with JQ1 (COPD + HIIT + JQ1 vs. COPD + HIIT). The protein expression level of TOMM20 also decreased after the addition of JQ1 injection, compared with that in the group with only HIIT intervention (Fig. 5b-d). These results further suggest that BRD4 may be involved in the regulation of mitochondrial function when it exerts its molecular function in repairing gastrocnemius muscle function. Subsequently, we performed HE staining on the gastrocnemius muscle tissue of each group to observe phenotypic changes. The results showed that compared with tissues without JQ1 injection, the gastrocnemius muscle tissue of rats that underwent high-intensity interval training (HIIT) with JQ1 injection exhibited restorative myofibril curling and rupture, as well as decreased nuclear dispersion (COPD + HIIT + JQ1 group vs. COPD + HIIT group) (Fig. 5e left). On the other hand, IHC results showed that in COPD rats that received HIIT, the gastrocnemius tissues showed a marked increase in MyHCI-positive staining and a corresponding decrease in MyHCIIb-positive staining (vs. COPD); however, upon JQ1 administration to rats subjected to HIIT, the MyHCI-positive tissue areas, which had exhibited recovery relative to the COPD group, underwent a renewed reduction, whereas the MyHCIIb-positive tissue areas, previously suppressed compared to the COPD group, displayed a modest increase again. (Fig. 5e right). The quantified fibre type (%) was presented as the positive cross sectional area / total cross sectional area which was calulated using ImageJ (Fig. 5f). The results suggest that inhibiting BRD4 with JQ1 weakened the positive effects of HIIT to some extent, indicating that BRD4 is one of the molecular targets of HIIT in alleviating COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction. Finally, the changes in mitochondrial membrane potential in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues of four groups of rats were evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (green fluorescence: Ex = 490 nm; red fluorescence: Ex = 525 nm). The results showed that, compared with COPD rats without HIIT, the fluorescence Readout Ratio (red/green) of JC-1 in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues of COPD rats that underwent HIIT was significantly increased, indicating that HIIT partially restored mitochondrial damage (COPD + HIIT vs. COPD). Additionally, it was observed that in rats that underwent HIIT with JQ1 injection, the fluorescence Readout Ratio (red/green) in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues decreased again (COPD + HIIT + JQ1 vs. COPD + HIIT), suggesting that the high-affinity BRD4 inhibitor JQ1 could partially reverse the repair of mitochondrial damage in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues of COPD rats induced by HIIT. These findings suggest that mitochondrial regulation plays an important role in the improvement of gastrocnemius muscle function by HIIT in COPD rats, and BRD4 is one of the key targets for the positive therapeutic effects of HIIT (Fig. 5g).

caption.p: BRD4 mediates the effects of high-intensity interval training (HIIT) on alleviating COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction. (a) HIIT improves training duration (left), hindlimb muscle strength (middle), and relative gastrocnemius muscle weight (right) in COPD rats. The beneficial effects of HIIT are attenuated by the BRD4 inhibitor JQ1. Data are presented as mean ± SD; n = 5 per group. ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 between indicated groups. (b) Relative mRNA levels of BRD4 and PGC-1α in gastrocnemius muscle tissues. Data are presented as mean ± SD; n = 3 per group. ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 between indicated groups. (c) Relative mRNA levels of MYH7, MYH2, MYH4 and TOMM20 in gastrocnemius muscle tissues. Data are presented as mean ± SD; n = 3 per group. **p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001 vs.COPD group; #p < 0.01, ####p < 0.0001 vs.COPD+HIIT group. (d) The results of western blot analysis. (e) (left) HE staining of COPD lung tissues and gastrocnemius muscle tissues in COPD rats with different treatment at 10× magnification. Scale bar = 100 μm; (right) IHC staining of MyHCI and MyHCIIb in gastrocnemius muscle tissues of COPD rats with different treatment at 20× magnification. Scale bar = 50 μm. (f) percentage of IHC-positive cross-sectional area relative to total tissue cross-sectional area, *P < 0.05,***P < 0.001,****p < 0.0001 between indicated groups. (g) Mitochondrial membrane potential assessed by JC-1 fluorescence in gastrocnemius muscle tissues. HIIT increases the red fluorescence (indicating higher membrane potential) readout / the green fluorescence (indicating lower membrane potential) readout Ratio. Data are presented as mean ± SD; n = 5 per group. ****p < 0.0001 between indicated groups

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: COPD is considered a systemic inflammatory disease implicating a wide range of cytokines and chemokines (Henrot et al. 2019) and including several comorbidities, among which skeletal muscle wasting is a prevalent and severe condition and is responsible for substantial morbidity and mortality (Henrot et al. 2023). The origin of skeletal muscle wasting in COPD is a topic of debate, and in particular, no specific and efficient pharmacological therapy is available to date; therefore, exploring effective therapeutic strategies to improve skeletal muscle function in COPD patients is of great clinical significance. Over the past five years, research on the application of HIIT in patients with COPD has made significant progress. Studies have shown that HIIT is superior to MICT in enhancing both pulmonary function and skeletal muscle function in COPD patients. A Chinese clinical trial demonstrated that the HIIT group exhibited significantly higher peak power, exercise test duration, anaerobic threshold, peak oxygen uptake, peak ventilation, FEV1/FVC ratio, FEV1% of expected value, LVEF (%), and SF-36 scores compared to the MICT group but lower LVEDD and the incidence of COPD acute exacerbation than in the MICT group (Wang et al. 2025). Additionally, HIIT was more effective than MICT in improving cognitive function, cardiopulmonary endurance, and muscle strength in COPD patients (Martland et al. 2020). T These findings suggest that HIIT is an efficient and feasible rehabilitation modality that can better improve pulmonary and skeletal muscle function in COPD patients, with good patient tolerability.

Our results showed that HIIT significantly improved pulmonary function and gastrocnemius muscle function in COPD rats, as evidenced by increased FEV200, FVC, training duration, hindlimb muscle strength, and relative gastrocnemius weight. These findings are consistent with previous studies demonstrating the positive effects of HIIT on cardiopulmonary function and skeletal muscle performance in both healthy individuals and patients with various chronic diseases (Liu et al., 2022a; Tzanis et al. 2017). Moreover, our study revealed that HIIT led to a significant increase in the expression of oxidative myofiber heavy chain subtype markers (MyHCI and MyHCIIa) and a decrease in the glycolytic myofiber heavy chain subtype marker (MyHCIIb) in the gastrocnemius muscle tissue of COPD rats, indicating a favorable muscle fiber type transformation towards more oxidative and fatigue-resistant phenotypes. This muscle fiber type transformation is likely to contribute to the enhanced muscle function observed in HIIT-trained COPD rats.

However, it is regrettable that research on the molecular targets and mechanisms underlying the positive therapeutic effects of HIIT remains very limited. Owing to our limited experience, the gastrocnemius samples from COPD rats, including those subjected to HIIT, intended for transcriptomic sequencing, were not preserved correctly in accordance with quality-control requirements. In an attempt to preliminarily identify potential molecular targets of HIIT, we therefore had to turn to public databases for an alternative solution. Unfortunately, no raw data of skeletal muscle transcriptome sequencing following HIIT have been published. Then, we were only able to initially explore the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) just between the normal control (NC) and COPD rat gastrocnemius muscle transcriptomes, and subsequently validated the genes of interest in cellular models and gastrocnemius muscle tissues, with particular focus on those tissues that had undergone HIIT. During the bioinformatics analysis of the GSE data, we focused particularly on key signaling molecules involved in myofiber synthesis and transcriptional regulatory factors. Among these, the transcriptional activator BRD4 and peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma co-activator 1 alpha (PGC-1α) attracted our attention.

PGC-1α is one of the few molecules considered as a potential therapeutic target of HIIT (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al. 2023) which plays roles in governing oxidative myofiber specification. Its overexpression in porcine muscle augments type I and IIa fibers while depleting glycolytic IIb fibers, conferring fatigue resistance. PGC-1α is markedly reduced in skeletal muscle of COPD patients, implicating its down-regulation in COPD-associated myopathy (Gosker et al. 2019; Ying et al. 2016). Meanwhile, BRD4, which is involved in gene expression regulation and cell proliferation/ differentiation, has attracted significant attention in the field of cancer research but has not yet been explored in the context of SMS in COPD. BET proteins are found to be aberrantly expressed in COPD small-airway fibrosis, suggesting their involvement in disease pathogenesis (Zakarya et al. 2021). BRD4 inhibition could reduce PGC-1α transcription in PGC-1α-overexpressing melanoma cells, indicating direct transcriptional regulation (Gelato et al. 2018). However, whether BRD4 can influence the transcription of PGC in the gastrocnemius muscle of COPD remains unclear.

Bioinformatic analysis in our study revealed synchronous down-regulation of BRD4 and PGC-1α in the gastrocnemius muscle of the COPD rat with robust inter-gene correlation. We also verified that both BRD4 and PGC-1α were significantly downregulated in both COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle tissues and in CSE-treated L6 cells when compared with controls. Moreover, BRD4 overexpression in L6 cells could restore the expression of PGC-1α and regulate the myogenic subtype transformation by attenuating mitochondrial damage caused by cigarette smoke extract (CSE) treatment. More notably, HIIT intervention partially restored the markedly reduced expressions of BRD4 and PGC-1α in the gastrocnemius muscle of COPD rats. The results suggest that the BRD4/PGC-1α axis may be involved in the SMD pathogenesis in COPD and the therapeutic effects of HIIT.

To further explore the role of BRD4 in the effects of HIIT on alleviating COPD-induced gastrocnemius muscle dysfunction, the highly selective BRD4 inhibitor JQ1 was used in vivo experiments. The results showed that the beneficial effects of HIIT on training duration, hindlimb muscle strength, and relative gastrocnemius weight in COPD rats were significantly attenuated by JQ1 injection. Additionally, BRD4 inhibition by JQ1 resulted in a corresponding decrease in the expression of PGC-1α, which further underscores the intimate association between the two in our study context. MyHCI, MyHCIIa, and MyHCIIb in the gastrocnemius muscle tissues of COPD rats were also affected by JQ1 injection, indicating that BRD4 plays a pivotal role in regulating mitochondrial function and muscle fiber type transformation in skeletal muscle, and that the BRD4/PGC-1α axis plays an essential role in HIIT in improving gastrocnemius muscle function in COPD rats.

JQ1 is a highly selective BET (bromodomain and extra-terminal) family inhibitor with the highest affinity for BRD4 and is therefore often used as a “BRD4 inhibitor”. Previous studies have shown that JQ1 exerts beneficial effects both in vivo and in vitro COPD models, mainly by attenuating airway and lung inflammation, reducing the deposition of airway collagen 15α1 and tenascin-c, and suppressing M1/M2 polarization of alveolar macrophages, thereby mitigating emphysema and chronic inflammation (Duan et al. 2023; Li et al. 2025; Zakarya et al. 2021). In this study, JQ1 was employed as a BRD4 inhibitor to provide evidence that HIIT alleviates COPD-induced gastrocnemius dysfunction, at least in part, by modulating BRD4. However, when JQ1 was administered alone to COPD rats, it failed to significantly improve gastrocnemius function. This may be because JQ1 can mitigate certain pulmonary manifestations of COPD to some extent, yet its short-term efficacy against yet its short-term efficacy against extrapulmonary manifestations such as SMD, is limited. The findings of our study have ultimately revealed that HIIT significantly improves pulmonary function and gastrocnemius muscle function in COPD rats, and the BRD4/PGC-1α axis may play a crucial role in mediating the beneficial effects of HIIT. These results offer novel perspectives on the mechanisms behind the therapeutic impact of HIIT on skeletal muscle dysfunction caused by COPD and emphasize the possibility of targeting the BRD4/PGC-1α axis as a treatment for skeletal muscle dysfunction in COPD patients. Future research should delve deeper into the detailed molecular mechanisms by which HIIT regulates the BRD4/PGC-1α axis and investigate the potential clinical applications of targeting this axis for treating COPD-related skeletal muscle dysfunction.

sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: Not applicable.This research was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of Hunan (2022JJ40727).

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: Zhang Wanwan performed most of the conception and design, provision of study material by Yang Liting and Peng Wenn, collection and/or assembly of data by Zhu Chunjiao and Yang Liting, data analysis and interpretation by Zhu Chunjiao and Zhang Wanwan, and manuscript writing by Zhu Chunjiao and Zhang Wanwan. Final approval of the manuscript by Zhang Wanwan. All authors have read and approved the manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: National Natural Science Foundation of Hunan (2022JJ40727).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: All the original experimental results involved in this article can be obtained by contacting the corresponding author at the email address provided.

notes.title: Conflict of interest
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

notes.title: AI-generated work
notes.p: The authors declare that they have not used AI-generated work in this manuscript.

element-citation.article-title: The rat model of COPD skeletal muscle dysfunction induced by progressive cigarette smoke exposure: a pilot study
